name: Aditya Mudgal
headline: AI, Policy and Everything Else | ex-KPMG | Founder, Unbundled | Master's in Public Policy at National Law School, Bangalore
about:
I believe the world is not fixed, it can be reimagined and transformed in every fundamental way our imagination allows. To be part of ideas with the potential to reshape how we live is exciting, because the world we know today was built by people no smarter than us.

Technologies like AI, Renewable Energy, and Space Exploration are opening possibilities that continue to spark my curiosity about what the future can look like. I have always wanted to be a part of impactful technologies that have changed the world and in today's time I have put my bet with AI

experiences.location:
Leading strategic initiatives to scale AI and data-driven solutions with government bodies across India. 

Working across strategy, partnerships, and project management.
experiences.description:
Leading strategic initiatives to scale AI and data-driven solutions with government bodies across India. 

Working across strategy, partnerships, and project management.

Deployed as one of two consultants supporting key trade and economic initiatives within the Ministry of Commerce.

Highlights:

FTA Utilization Dashboard: Designed a data-driven dashboard with the FTA Division to enhance trade agreement utilization.

IPEF Supply Chain Agreement: Identified 71 vulnerable goods; conducted HHI analysis and developed a Goods Vulnerability Matrix.

5-Year Vision & 100-Day Plan: Supported strategy presented to the Hon’ble PM; projected $1.3T exports target and 3.8% global share by FY’29.

Services Export Strategy: Researched global trends, mapped target markets, and led stakeholder consultations for SEPC's vision.

Foreign Trade Policy 2023: Conducted global strategy benchmarking; led consultations with 20+ ministries and industry bodies.

experiences.location:
Unbundled is a platform with a public policy niche. It is a co-authorship platform, that connects interested individuals looking to author something together. 
Every week Unbundled publishes brief policy explainers called Policy Shorts.
experiences.description:

Every week Unbundled publishes brief policy explainers called Policy Shorts.

Worked on building partnerships with various organizations and taking part in the core strategy work. Supporting various departments in the organization like national G20 team. Assisting in donor landscaping and stakeholder identification. Preparing policy briefs, market analysis and regular health policy updates

Assisted Director of Public Policy, Richa Mukherjee directly. Engaged in collaborations with Digital India initiative, Common Service Centers to work on digital literacy and financial literacy. Planned the CSR and other company initiatives on Digital Literacy and Financial Inclusion.
Executed secondary research to work on reports of Digitizing rural payments. Assisted in drafting the charter for upcoming PayU Foundation with an eye for improving innovation at rural levels in ecommerce and financial support. Worked on strategizing the Do-Good Mission of
PayU’s global sustainability strategy and corporate relations.

Co-authored a book chapter titled “Towards In-Situ Frameworks for Research and Action on Mobile Instant Messaging Services”.

Presented the working paper at Center for Internet Society seminar on Empirical approaches to information disorder.

Researched critically about Indian government’s approach on traceability of communications, regulations regarding various technologies and contemporary issues regarding encryption-based communication platforms.

posts.content: All of us at Sarvam have been building toward this moment, and it’s finally here. Tomorrow would mark the beginning of a new era in the Indian tech sector.

It’s rare for a penal reform to test behavioral science, medical ethics, and jurisprudence all at once and yet this one does.In the quiet corners of sentencing reform, a decision is unfolding that speaks to both our aspirations for public safety and the dilemmas of ethical boundaries.The UK government has announced that a pilot program for voluntary chemical castration of sex offenders will be expanded to 20 prisons. Originally introduced in 2007 and extended in 2022, the treatment combines hormonal medications with psychological interventions to reduce the risk of reoffending, particularly among offenders with compulsive or intrusive sexual thoughts.The move is not just penal policy. It’s an experiment in behavioral change and a test of how policy can (or should) intervene in shaping human desire, risk, and restraint.Behavioral science tells us that long-term change rarely comes from punishment alone. The psychology behind some sexual offences often stems not only from impulse but also from power, control, and deeply entrenched patterns of thought. Interventions that reduce biological drive may help but only when paired with work that addresses these cognitive and emotional roots.The Independent Sentencing Review, led by former Lord Chancellor David Gauke, cautiously endorsed further study of this approach not as punishment, but as targeted therapy for a small group. Crucially, it warned that the intervention must not become a blanket risk management tool.But the government's willingness to explore making it mandatory suggests a tension. The foundation of behavioral intervention is voluntary engagement. Once consent is compromised whether through coercion, parole conditions, or pressure the integrity of both the treatment and the legal framework surrounding it becomes fragile.Jurisdictions like Germany and Denmark have kept this distinction intact. Others have not. England and Wales now sit at that fork in the road.There’s an opportunity here for evidence-led policy to take the lead one that respects autonomy, builds a nuanced understanding of reoffending, and holds the line between rehabilitation and compulsion. The data from this pilot will matter. But how we interpret it, and the guardrails we place around its application, may matter even more.In the end, the question is not only what works but what works ethically, sustainably, and humanely in the long arc of justice.Reference: https://lnkd.in/dqhUGNpy, https://lnkd.in/dEAVvxtf

🌈 A beautiful step forward.Recently, my friend Prateek Sharma organized something quietly revolutionary. Indian-style wedding celebration between two men. Not a legal marriage, not a political statement, but a celebration of love, dignity, and culture.In a world where progress often comes in headlines and court battles, this was something softer and perhaps more powerful. A moment of joy, rituals, families, sherwanis, and smiles. The kind of celebration that helps normalize what should’ve never been seen as abnormal.No loud activism. Just warmth, intention, and love.It reminded me that progress isn’t always a leap, sometimes it’s a gentle, beautiful step. The kind we need more of.Here’s to more of these moments. To those quietly shaping a more inclusive world, one celebration at a time.
